coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric visual detection of tetracycline residues in milk by framework-enhanced fluorescence of gold and copper nanoclusters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Khataee, Alireza
Jalili, Roghayeh
Dastborhan, Mahsa
Karimi, Afzal
Ebadi Fard Azar, Amirali
coredata.dc:description: We reported a novel dual-emission fluorescence probe fabricated by encapsulating both gold (AuNCs) and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). Obtained composite (AuCuNCs@MOF) was utilized for ratiometric determination of Tetracycline (Tcy) antibiotic. Under excitation at 400 nm, the AuCuNCs@MOF composite displayed two emission peaks at 520 and 650 nm which were originated from AuNCs and CuNCs, respectively. Upon the addition of Tcy, the red emission intensity of the nanoparticles at 615 nm was significantly decreased, while the green emission at 520 nm stayed almost constant which resulted in a clear fluorescence color change from red to green under a UV lamp. The logarithm of the fluorescence ratio against the concentration of Tcy exhibited a satisfactory linear relationship from 20 to 650 nM with a detection limit (LOD) of 4.8 nM. Current probe was applied for Tcy quantification in milk samples with superior results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Gold nanoclusters
Metal-organic frameworks
Ratiometric sensor
Tetracycline

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of AuCu NCs@ZIF-8
Determination of Tcy in milk samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the University of Tabriz and Iran University of Medical Sciences for all the support provided (Project No: 97-4-75-13659 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) AuNCs, (b) CuNCs and (c) AuCuNCs@ZIF-8. The inset image shows the digital photographs of (right) pure AuNCs, (left) AuNCs@ZIF-8 under ultraviolet light of 365 nm.
(a) HR-TEM image of AuNCs, (b) HR-TEM image of CuNCs, (c) TEM image of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8, (d) SEM image of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 and (e) HR-TEM image of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8.
XRD patterns of pure ZIF-8 (black pattern) and AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 (red pattern).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 probe upon the exposure of various concentrations of Tcy, (b) calibration regression curve (The experimental conditions were λex = 400 nm, pH = 7 and incubation times = 5 min) and (c) color changes of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probe upon the addition of different concentrations of Tcy (from left to right respectively: 0, 50, 100, 300, and 600 nM) under an ultraviolet lamp.
Determination of Tcy using the ratiometric MOF based probe.
Comparison of different fluorescent methods for Tcy sensing.
Results for the determination of Tcy in raw and pasteurized milk samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric visual detection of tetracycline residues in milk by framework-enhanced fluorescence of gold and copper nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Medicine, Iran University of Medical Sciences, 14496-14535 Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Medicine, Iran University of Medical Sciences, 14496-14535 Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported a novel dual-emission fluorescence probe fabricated by encapsulating both gold (AuNCs) and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). Obtained composite (AuCuNCs@MOF) was utilized for ratiometric determination of Tetracycline (Tcy) antibiotic. Under excitation at 400 nm, the AuCuNCs@MOF composite displayed two emission peaks at 520 and 650 nm which were originated from AuNCs and CuNCs, respectively. Upon the addition of Tcy, the red emission intensity of the nanoparticles at 615 nm was significantly decreased, while the green emission at 520 nm stayed almost constant which resulted in a clear fluorescence color change from red to green under a UV lamp. The logarithm of the fluorescence ratio against the concentration of Tcy exhibited a satisfactory linear relationship from 20 to 650 nM with a detection limit (LOD) of 4.8 nM. Current probe was applied for Tcy quantification in milk samples with superior results.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (Tcy), an effective antibiotic is extensively utilized in veterinary practice for the treatment of bacterial infection in animals thanks to its inclusive activity against pathogenic microorganisms, satisfying oral absorption and low cost [1,2]. In the long run, the residues of Tcy in food stuffs can cause the development and distribution of bacterial resistance to antibiotics. The residues can also promote other problems to the consumers, such as allergic reactions, gastrointestinal side effects and teeth discoloration [3]. To avoid the negative health effects, the European Union (EU) has recommended a maximum residue limit (MRL) of 100 ppb for Tcy in milk (the Commission Regulation No. 37/2010) [4]. Currently, different methods including chromatographic methods [5], chemiluminescence [6], electrochemical sensors [7] and more have been developed for the determination of Tcy. Most of these techniques suffer from more or fewer defects due to the sophisticated instruments or difficult sample preparation processes. The development of quick, efficient and sensitive quantification for point-of-care testing (POCT) of tetracycline in food samples is highly desirable but remains challenging.

Ratiometric fluorescent (RF) technique, in which the fluorescence intensity ratio at two well-resolved wavelengths is measured to detect changes to analyte concentrations, provides a built-in correction against external interferences. Thus circumvent false signals origination from environmental effects and improve the signal accuracy and provide high sensitivity with typically detection limits below 100 nM. Importantly, the changes in fluorescence intensities ratio lead to consecutive color change which is distinguishable enough to be observed by the naked eye and can be used in point of care testing [8,9].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a category of highly ordered crystalline materials, are made by linking of a regular array of positively charged metal ions and organic ligands. MOFs structure hyphenates the intrinsic advantages of rigid inorganic and flexible organic materials [10]. The most appealing property of MOFs is their porosity that allows the diffusion of guest molecules into the bulk structure. Thus MOFs have been applied as ideal host matrices to encapsulate diverse guests, such as enzymes [11], polymers [12], biomolecules [13] and various nanomaterials [14–16]. Incorporating guest materials into MOF structure yields functionalized MOF-based composites with unparalleled and better properties and performance.

As a promising substitute for organic dyes and quantum dots, fluorescent metal nanoclusters have attractive unique size-dependent features including easy functionalization, excellent biocompatibility which make them ideal candidates for signal generation and transduction in biological and chemical sensing applications [17]. However, their relatively low quantum yields (QY) have limited their application in sensing and imaging [18,19]. Most of the current methods for the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of metal NCs are based on strategies like aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [14,20,21], crystallization [22] and restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) [23–25]. Thus, developing novel biocompatible, simple and cheap methods to increase the fluorescence intensity of metal nanoclusters is of great importance.

In this work we devised a fluorescent sensing strategy by encapsulating of AuNCs and Cu NCs into ZIF-8 structure for the trace level detection of Tcy. Obtained nanocomposite exhibited two distinct emission peaks at 650 nm and 520 nm under a single excitation. When exposed to different concentration of Tcy, the red fluorescence (F620) was quenched gradually and used as the response signal, whereas the green fluorescence (F520) remained almost stable and acted as the reference signal. The logarithm of fluorescence intensity ratio is applied as a read out signal for constructing a ratiometric probe for Tcy sensing. The fluorescence intensity ratio variation results in an obviously distinguishable fluorescence color variation from red to green which could be observed by the naked eye. Additionally, the proposed ratiometric probe was applied successfully for the determination of Tcy in milk samples (Scheme 1 ).

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 10,000), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 2-methylimidazole were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (US). Tetrachloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.99%), amino acids, CuCl2·2H2O and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Merck (Germany). Tetracycline (Tcy) was obtained from Dana Pharmaceutical Co (Tabriz, Iran).

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer LS45 fluorimeter (USA). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption results were acquired using Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer (Japan). The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and Brunauere Emmette Teller (BET) surface area were performed by Quantachrome C Instrument (Chem BET-3000, Quantachrome, USA). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) performed by JEOL (JEM-2200FS, Japan) TEM operated at 200 kV. Morphology and particle size distribution of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostracture analyzed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Mira 3 FEG, Tescan Co., Czech Republic). The dynamic light scattering of AuNCs before and after mixing with Tcy were measured on a Malvern Mastersizer (UK) instrument.

The CuNCs were synthesized by a facile method. PVP and AA were used as template and reductant respectively [26]. Briefly, 0.2 g PVP, 300 μL AA (0.1 M), 100 μL CuCl2 (50 mM) and 10 mL ultrapure water were loaded into a 25-mL two-necked flask then the air was replaced with N2. The reaction system was sealed and kept under stirring at 90 °C for 21 h. The color of the solution changed from colorless to orange-yellow, suggesting the formation of CuNCs. The obtained CuNCs were kept in refrigerator.

As reported in literature, BSA not only can reduce gold ion but also serves as a stabilizing agent against the aggregation [27]. Firstly, aqueous HAuCl4 solution (2.5 mL, 10 mM) was added to BSA solution (2.5 mL, 75 mM) and stirred for 5 min. The pH was adjusted at 11.0 using NaOH solution (0.5 mL, 1 M). Then the resultant solution was incubated at 37 °C for 8 h. The color of the solution switched from pale yellow to brown gradually. Then the pH of the AuNCs was adjusted at 7.0 and it was kept in refrigerator.

The AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures were synthesized using a procedure that we have described before [14]. First, 10 mL of Zn(NO3)2.6H2O (0.987 mmol) solution was mixed with 10 mL of 2-methylimidazole (7.904 mmol) solution and the mixture was stirred vigorously. Synthesized ZIF-8 nanoparticles were separated by centrifuge and washed thoroughly with water. ZIF-8 nanoparticles were redispersed in 50 mL water and then 5 mL of AuNCs and 5 mL of CuNCs solution was added and continued stirring for 2 h. Resultant AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure was separated and washed with water and then dispersed in 100 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.01 M, pH = 7).

In a typical assay, 100 μL of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 suspension and 500 μL of PBS (pH = 7, 0.01 M) were mixed in 5 mL volumetric flask. Then certain concentrations of Tcy solution were added and the volume was diluted to 5 mL with water. After equilibration for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 500–700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

Raw and pasteurized cow milk samples were purchased from supermarkets. A 1.0 mL aliquot of each milk sample was spiked by adding appropriate amounts of Tcy standard solution. To remove proteins, 100 μL of trichloroacetic acid (10% w/w) was added into spiked samples. The mixture was centrifuged for 20 min, the supernatant was diluted to 10 times using PBS (pH = 7, 0.01 M) and analyzed by ratiometric probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of AuCu NCs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: Red emitting AuNCs were synthesized via a fast reduction process employing BSA as both reductant and protecting ligand. The as-prepared AuNCs exhibit relatively weak fluorescence peak centered at 650 nm (Fig. 1 , curve A). The quantum yield of the AuNCs in water was 1.1% (fluorescein, QY = 97% as the standard, at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm). CuNCs was prepared using PVP as a template and AA as a mild reductant. At the excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the as-prepared CuNCs display a strong green fluorescence at 520 nm (Fig. 1, curve B). The QY of the CuNCs was determined to be 40.32% with Rhodamine 6G (QY = 0.95 in ethanol) as a reference. When excited at 400 nm, AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure showed two clear fluorescence peaks at 520 and 650 nm (Fig. 1, curve C). Sole ZIF-8 didn't exhibit any distinct fluorescence peak. Based on this results, it can be concluded that the fluorescence characteristics of CuNCs and AuNCs are well maintained after encapsulating in MOF and the fluorescence of the AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 is emitted from the nanoclusters rather than ZIF-8.

In order to prove the fluorescence enhancement of AuNCs by encapsulation within the ZIF-8 structure and to investigate the contribution of the host-guest effects, we measured the fluorescence intensities of AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure. As the inset in Fig. 1 shows, when illuminated by UV light, the sole AuNCs displayed a pale red luminescence while AuNCs@ZIF-8 exhibited a brilliant red luminescence. The QYs of AuNCs and AuNCs@ZIF-8 were determined to be 1.1% and 17.14%, respectively. The QY enhancement (about 15 times) in the AuNCs@ZIF-8 is attributed to the framework confinement and protection of the MOF for AuNCs. Due to MOFs skeleton that exhibited a space limiting effect, AuNCs were firmly entrapped by ZIF-8, which remarkably restrict the vibrations of BSA ligands thus the most of excited electrons back down into ground state by the radiative relaxation rather than nonradiative relaxation, resulting in fluorescence intensity amplification [21,28]. ZIF-8 combines the properties of two materials, zeolites and MOFs, including high porosity, high surface area and chemical resistance to harsh circumstances.

As shown in the UV–Vis spectra of AuNCs in Fig. S1, the absorption peak at ~280 nm is attributed to the absorbance of aromatic amino acid residues of BSA, also there is another absorption peak at 500 nm indicating that the particles size are less than 2.5 nm [29,30]. In the UV–Vis spectrum of CuNCs, no plasmon resonance peak observed in the range of 500–600 nm (Fig. S2), which suggested the nanocluster size level and lack of larger copper nanoparticles. The morphology and size of AuNCs and CuNCs were characterized by HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a and b) respectively, both AuNCs and CuNCs display a quasi-spherical shape and the average diameter was about 2–3 nm for both AuNCs and CuNCs. TEM image (Fig. 2c) illustrated that the AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures have the rhombic dodecahedron shape and the mean size of them is 60 nm approximately. As shown in SEM image (Fig. 2d), the AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures have monodisperse size distribution and the particle sizes are in the range of 40 and 80 nm. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 2e) of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 confirmed that NCs (black dots) have been confined in MOF structure with good dispersity. There are no free nanoclusters, suggesting successful encapsulation of nanoclusters.

The crystal structures of pure ZIF-8 and AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 were characterized by XRD. As shown in XRD patterns (Fig. 3 ), both the ZIF-8 and AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 display the reflection at 2θ = 7.3° related to a 110 peak which suggests that the crystal structures of ZIF-8 and AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 are composed of 12 [110] facets and exhibit rhombic dodecahedron-shaped crystals. It can be found that pure ZIF-8 crystals and AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 XRD patterns are agreed well with each other which indicates that due to NCs low amount and small sizes, encapsulation of AuNCs and CuNCs didn't cause the collapse of ZIF-8 crystal structure [29,30]. The porosity of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 was studied using BET method. The AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 displayed a typical Type-I BET isotherm indicating the microporous nature of the AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures (Fig. S3). The BET surface area of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure was 324.8 m2 g−1 which is lower than reported value for ZIF-8 nanocrystals [31]. This indicates that the cavities of ZIF-8 are partly occupied by NCs.

To increase the sensitivity of this ratiometric sensor, some experimental parameters for Tcy detection, including pH and reaction time, were optimized. The effect of pH value on the interaction between Tcy and AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 was investigated in the pH range of 4.0–9.0. As shown in Fig. S4, the change of the fluorescence intensity ratio of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 [(F650/F520)0 / (F650/F520) where (F650/F520)0 and (F650/F520) are the fluorescence intensity ratio of the AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 composite in the absence and presence of Tcy respectively] reached a maximum at pH 7. Therefore, pH was adjusted to 7 by 0.01 M PBS as the optimal pH in the further experiments.

Finally, the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity ratio of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 in the presence of 150 nM of Tcy was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity ratio increased rapidly with increasing time in the initial 5 min and then reached a plateau indicating that the reaction between Tcy and AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 was very rapid and taking 5 min or less to reach completion.

As shown in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNCs at 650 nm gradually decreased upon the addition of Tcy whereas the green fluorescence of CuNCs at 520 nm remained almost constant. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities is proportional to concentration of Tcy. According to the Fig. 4b, the logarithm of fluorescence intensity ratio [log (F650/F520)0 / (F650/F520)] is linear as a function of the Tcy concentration in the range of 20–650 nM. The obtained linear equation (y = 0.0013C + 0.0019, R2 = 0.9975) was used for the quantification of Tcy. The LOD was calculated to be as low as 4.8 nM (3S/N). Apparently, this LOD is much lower than the recommended MRL concentration (225 nM) according to the commission regulation (EU) No. 37/2010. As the intensity ratio of the two fluorescence peaks are varied, the fluorescence color of the probe solution changed distinguishably from red to green (Fig. 4c) which was easily observed by the naked eye, thus a method for visual detection of Tcy was provided. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 10 measurements was 0.6%, which demonstrates the good reproducibility of method. Compared with the previous fluorescent methods for Tcy determination, the probe presents superior detection performance (Table 1 ).

The interactions of Tcy with BSA were studied previously exploiting different methods [37,38]. The interaction of Tcy and BSA ligands on the surface of AuNCs is main explanation for the quenching mechanism. When Tcy bind to BSA on the AuNCs surface, the physiochemical environment of AuNCs is changed as a result which decrease the level of radioactive decay. As shown in Fig. S6, the strong interaction of BSA and Tcy was confirmed via the significant quenching of the BSA fluorescence (centered at 350 nm) in the presence of various concentration of Tcy. Also based on DLS analysis (Fig. S7), the size of AuNCs get larger after interaction with Tcy [39].

To show the superiority of AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probe over single wavelength sensing method, fluorescence quenching experiment for determination of Tcy was carried out using sole AuNCs. The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs, decreased with increasing Tcy concentration and there was a good linear relationship between (F 0-F)/F 0 and an excellent linearity from 180 to 900 nM was correspondingly obtained. The LOD was calculated to be 85 nM. The sensitivity of AuNCs was significantly lower than AuCuNCs@ZIF-8 ratiometric prob. The first reason for high sensitivity is ratiometric technique, which can reduce the interference caused by the external environment to a great extent and creates particular sensitivity. Also MOF scaffold provides not only higher surface areas and enhanced fluorescence intensity, but also shape/size selectivity which is important for sensing application.

To evaluate the anti-interference ability of the composite for sensing Tcy in milk samples, interference experiments were carried out in the presence of Tcy (150 nM) together with some potentially interfering substances such as metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), saccharides (Glucose, Lactose, Fructose) and amino acids (Tryptophan, Alanine, Glycine and Tyrosine). Fig. S8 indicates that F650/F520 response exhibited no obvious changes in the presence of excess amounts of these substances (relative errors not larger than 5%). The negligible interference from other substances suggests the applicability of the probe for detection of Tcy in real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Tcy in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the applicability of the proposed method, our ratiometric probe was adapted to detect Tcy concentration in raw and pasteurized milk samples. A series of samples with concentrations of 200 (less than MRL value), 300 and 500 nM were obtained and then recovery studies were performed. As shown in Table 2 , the excellent results (recoveries ranging from 98.0% to 101.7% and RSD values less than 0.8) confirm that the application of the proposed ratiometric probe in biological sample analysis was anticipated to be promising.

In summary, a dual-emission ratiometric sensor based on the encapsulation of AuNCs and CuNCs into MOF structure was designed for the detection of Tcy in milk. Due to high selectivity and sensitivity, it can detect Tcy in milk samples at concentrations less than MRL value. Furthermore, the ability of sensor for visual detection of Tcy, make it possible to carry out point of care testing. The proposed method exhibited an excellent analytical performance as compared to traditional single emission methods. It is believed that this research work has opened another avenue for the point of care detection of antibiotics in the foodstuffs.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Alireza Khataee: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Roghayeh Jalili: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Mahsa Dastborhan: Investigation. Afzal Karimi: Resources, Formal analysis. Amirali Ebadi Fard Azar: Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the University of Tabriz and Iran University of Medical Sciences for all the support provided (Project No: 97-4-75-13659).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118715.
